summary: Stancil argues that data orchestration is backwards: we schedule pipelines from upstream triggers instead of from the freshness guarantees we actually care about at the end of the lineage. He proposes a reverse-ETL-style model where each table declares latency requirements and the system derives the DAG and run timing to meet those guarantees, improving maintainability, cost control, and alert relevance.
money_quotes: We want the updated datasets or fresh dashboards at the end of our lineage graph, but define our schedules around what’s at the beginning of it.

As analysts, we committed parameterized SQL statements and YAML configuration files to Integritie, which would use the queries, and the dependencies implied in them, to regularly create and update tables in our warehouse.
Instead, for each model, analysts set a latency requirement—this table should never have data that’s more than four hours out of date, a day out of date, a week out of date.
When adding a new table that we needed to update once a day, we didn’t have to define a procedure for making it so... We just told the tool what we wanted.
I just want my data to be fresh—where I can declare what fresh means—and to know when it’s not.
key_insight: Define data freshness guarantees first and let orchestration derive the DAG and schedules, rather than chaining tasks from upstream triggers.
